source_paper_title: Liquid Welfare Guarantees for No-Regret Learning in Sequential Budgeted Auctions
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Fikioris_2025, title={Liquid Welfare Guarantees for No-Regret Learning in Sequential Budgeted Auctions}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0274}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0274}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Fikioris, Giannis and Tardos, Éva}, year={2025}, month=may, pages={1233–1249} }
context:
Page 18, Section 8 (Conclusion)

While we prove positive liquid welfare guarantees under mild learning assumptions, we leave open the problem of whether stronger learning guarantees imply better liquid welfare. For example, can we get better liquid welfare guarantees if the players have high competitive ratio with respect to the best Lipschitz continuous function that maps values to bids? Or if the values are sampled from a distribution instead of being picked adversarially?
title: Design learning guarantees that improve liquid welfare in sequential budgeted auctions
background_latex:
Consider a sequential auction with one item per round over rounds \(t\in[T]=\{1,\dots,T\}\) and players \(i\in[n]\). Player \(i\) has a hard budget \(B_i>0\) and per-round value \(v_{it}\in[0,1]\) for winning the round-\(t\) item. (In the additive-valuation model, the value of a set of won rounds \(S\subseteq[T]\) is \(\sum_{t\in S} v_{it}\).) In each round players submit bids; in a first-price auction the highest bidder wins and pays her bid. Let \(V_i\) and \(P_i\) denote player \(i\)'s total realized value and payment over all rounds, and let the (budgeted quasilinear) utility be \(U_i = V_i-P_i\) if \(P_i\le B_i\) and \(-\infty\) otherwise.

The system performance metric is liquid welfare. Player \(i\)'s liquid welfare contribution is
\[\mathrm{LW}_i := \min\{B_i, V_i\},\qquad \mathrm{LW}:=\sum_{i=1}^n \mathrm{LW}_i.\]
Let \(\mathrm{LW}^*\) denote the maximum liquid welfare over all feasible allocations of items across rounds.

Players are assumed to be learning (not necessarily converging to equilibrium). A baseline behavioral benchmark studied in the paper is comparison to the best fixed multiplicative shading multiplier \(\lambda\in[0,1]\) applied to values each round, subject to not exceeding the remaining budget. More generally, one can consider richer benchmark classes mapping values to bids; e.g., a (scalar) bidding rule \(f:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) that is Lipschitz continuous (with some Lipschitz constant \(L\)) and induces bids \(b_{it}=f(v_{it})\) each round, again subject to the remaining budget. One can also distinguish adversarially chosen value sequences \((v_{it})\) versus values drawn i.i.d. from a distribution (stochastic model).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exist stronger, still natural learning-style performance guarantees on bidders' utilities that imply strictly better worst-case approximation of optimal liquid welfare \(\mathrm{LW}^*\) in sequential budgeted first-price auctions than the bounds obtained under comparison to the best fixed shading multiplier.

In particular, analyze the liquid-welfare price of anarchy achievable when each player attains a small competitive ratio (or no-regret) with respect to richer benchmark classes such as:
1) the best Lipschitz continuous bidding function \(f:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) mapping values to bids, and/or
2) the best fixed shading multiplier when the values \((v_{it})\) are sampled from a distribution rather than chosen adversarially.

The goal is to characterize the best provable factor \(\alpha\) (as a function of the learning guarantee parameters) such that \(\mathrm{LW} \ge \mathrm{LW}^*/\alpha\) (up to lower-order additive terms, if unavoidable) under these stronger learning assumptions.

open_problem_quote: While we prove positive liquid welfare guarantees under mild learning assumptions, we leave open the problem of whether stronger learning guarantees imply better liquid welfare. For example, can we get better liquid welfare guarantees if the players have high competitive ratio with respect to the best Lipschitz continuous function that maps values to bids? Or if the values are sampled from a distribution instead of being picked adversarially?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: For any proposed improved guarantee, one can search over small numbers of players/rounds, budgets, and value sequences to try to find instances where high performance against a richer benchmark still yields poor liquid welfare. However, the benchmark involves optimizing over a function class (e.g., Lipschitz functions), which makes automated checking more complex than for a finite action set.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects (adversarial instances, value distributions, or learning guarantees/bounds) can be represented programmatically, and a fitness signal can be based on computed liquid-welfare ratios under simulated learning behavior constrained to satisfy a benchmark-competitiveness inequality. But enforcing or optimizing over Lipschitz function benchmarks and quantifying worst-case guarantees is computationally heavy and may require relaxations or proxy optimization.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Fikioris--Tardos) establishes that in sequential budgeted first-price auctions, a per-bidder learning guarantee relative to the best fixed shading multiplier \(\lambda\in[0,1]\) implies a liquid-welfare approximation \(\mathrm{LW} \ge \mathrm{LW}^*/\alpha\) with \(\alpha=\gamma+\tfrac12+O(1/\gamma)\) (additive valuations), and nearly matching lower bounds \(\Omega(\max\{\gamma,2\})\). The open direction is whether strengthening the behavioral benchmark (e.g., to Lipschitz value-to-bid maps) and/or assuming stochastic i.i.d. values can strictly improve worst-case liquid-welfare guarantees, especially in the \(\gamma\approx 1\) regime where the paper’s bounds leave a gap between \(2\) and \(\approx 2.41\).

Forward citations provide partial progress along two axes. On the stochastic side, "Budget pacing in repeated auctions: Regret and efficiency without convergence" proves that gradient-based pacing under i.i.d. value profiles guarantees expected liquid welfare at least half of the optimal ex ante liquid welfare (up to \(\tilde O(n\sqrt{T})\) additive loss) without requiring convergence, matching the familiar factor-2 barrier in expectation. On the richer-benchmark learning side, "No-regret algorithms in non-truthful auctions with budget and roi constraints" shows that in i.i.d. environments one can in fact achieve no-regret (full information) against the best \(L\)-Lipschitz bidding function, substantially strengthening what is algorithmically feasible beyond fixed multipliers; however, it does not connect such guarantees to market-level liquid welfare when all bidders learn.

Several related works reinforce both possibilities and barriers: equilibrium-based analyses in autobidding models with budgets show factor-2-type liquid-welfare guarantees under additional assumptions (e.g., \(v\le B\)) or under pacing-equilibrium abstractions, while other results show large inefficiency is possible in deterministic first-price mechanisms absent such assumptions, and computational hardness suggests that selecting outcomes beating factor \(2-\epsilon\) can be intractable in related settings. Overall, the open problem remains unresolved: existing stochastic-learning results achieve \(1/2\)-optimal expected liquid welfare under pacing, and richer benchmark regret is achievable for single bidders, but a general theorem translating Lipschitz-benchmark no-regret (or i.i.d.-value assumptions) into strictly better worst-case liquid-welfare approximation for sequential budgeted first-price auctions has not yet been established.

sources.title: Budget pacing in repeated auctions: Regret and efficiency without convergence
sources.theorem_or_result:
Main aggregate-efficiency guarantee: when all agents use (generalized) gradient-based pacing (Algorithm 1) in any core auction with i.i.d. value profiles across rounds (allowing arbitrary within-round correlation), the expected liquid welfare of the induced allocation rule x satisfies
\[W(x,F) \ge \tfrac12 W(y,F) - O\big(n v\sqrt{T\log(v n T)}\big)\]
for every single-round allocation rule \(y:[0,v]^n\to X\) (Theorem 3.4), where \(W(\cdot,F)\) is ex ante liquid welfare. In particular, taking y to be an optimal allocation rule yields a \(2\)-approximation to optimal expected liquid welfare up to an additive \(\tilde O(n v\sqrt T)\) term, without requiring convergence of the learning dynamics.
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether stronger learning guarantees (e.g., regret vs richer benchmark classes like Lipschitz bidding functions, or stochastic values) can imply strictly better worst-case approximation of optimal liquid welfare than guarantees obtained when comparing to the best fixed shading multiplier. This paper directly addresses the stochastic-values variant: it assumes values are drawn i.i.d. across rounds and analyzes simultaneous learning via pacing multipliers (a fixed linear shading form). It proves a constant-factor liquid-welfare guarantee (\(\ge 1/2\) of optimal expected liquid welfare, up to \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) additive loss) that holds along the entire learning trajectory (no convergence needed) and for first-price auctions (indeed any core auction). However, it does not improve the approximation factor beyond \(1/2\), nor does it analyze regret against richer benchmark classes such as Lipschitz value-to-bid functions; the individual regret results are primarily against pacing-multiplier benchmarks (best fixed multiplier in stochastic settings; perfect pacing sequence in adversarial settings). Thus it provides substantial progress on part (2) of the open problem (stochastic model) but not on part (1) (richer benchmarks) and not on achieving a strictly better worst-case factor than the fixed-multiplier-based bounds.

sources.title: No-regret algorithms in non-truthful auctions with budget and roi constraints
sources.theorem_or_result:
In a stochastic repeated-auction model with i.i.d. draws \((v_t,d_t)\in[0,1]^2\) (value and highest competing bid), a single budget \(B=\rho T\) and ROI constraint, and first-price or more generally hybrid pricing \(p(b,d)=q b+(1-q)d\) satisfying Assumption 3.1, the paper gives no-regret algorithms against the benchmark OPT defined as the best (possibly randomized) \(L\)-Lipschitz bidding function \(f:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) chosen with knowledge of the distribution. In particular:
- (Full information) Theorem 4.1 (informal Thm 1.1): an algorithm with full-information feedback achieves \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) regret vs OPT while satisfying budget and ROI constraints (ROI exactly via a reduction), with regret scaling as \(\frac{1}{\beta^2\rho}\,O\big(\sqrt{L T\log T}+\sqrt{T\log(1/\delta)}\big)\) under a slack parameter \(\beta>0\) (Eq. (2)).
- (Bandit, first-price) Theorem 5.1: any bandit-feedback algorithm can incur regret \(\Omega(T^{2/3})\) even with constant value \(v_t\equiv 1\) and budget \(\rho=1/4\).
- (Bandit upper bound) Theorem 5.2: a polynomial-time bandit algorithm achieves regret \(\tilde O\big(\frac{1}{\beta\rho}(L^{1/4}T^{3/4}+T^{1/2}L^{1/2})\big)\) (and can be made ROI-exact with extra \(1/\beta\) factor under conditions).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks whether stronger learning guarantees—especially no-regret vs richer benchmarks like Lipschitz bidding functions and/or in stochastic (i.i.d.) environments—can imply strictly better worst-case liquid-welfare approximation in sequential budgeted first-price auctions than guarantees based only on best fixed shading multipliers.

This paper directly advances the learning side of that question in the stochastic model: it replaces the weak “best constant pacing/shading multiplier” benchmark with the much richer class of \(L\)-Lipschitz value-to-bid maps, and proves near-optimal \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) regret (full information) under budget/ROI constraints. Thus it establishes that such stronger learning guarantees are achievable (algorithmically) in first-price/hybrid auctions.

However, it studies a single bidder’s regret relative to OPT and does not analyze multi-bidder equilibrium/price-of-anarchy style implications for liquid welfare \(\mathrm{LW}\) versus \(\mathrm{LW}^*\). In particular, it does not prove a liquid-welfare PoA bound (as a function of the Lipschitz-regret parameters) for sequential first-price auctions when all bidders run these algorithms. Therefore it does not resolve the open problem’s welfare-characterization goal, but provides a key ingredient (no-regret-to-Lipschitz benchmark in the i.i.d. model) that could potentially be plugged into a welfare analysis.

sources.title: Efficiency of non-truthful auctions in auto-bidding with budget constraints
sources.theorem_or_result:
In the auto-bidding model with return-on-spend (ROS) and hard budget constraints, the paper proves price-of-anarchy bounds for several (single-shot, per-query) first-price-style mechanisms with respect to optimal liquid welfare (allowing randomized/fractional allocations). Key theorems include:
- For deterministic first-price auction (FPA), 
  - 
  \[\mathrm{PoA}(\mathrm{FPA}) = n\] 
  in the worst case (Theorems 3.1–3.3), when benchmarking against the optimal randomized allocation.
  - Under the assumption \(v_{i,j}\le B_i\) for all bidders/queries, 
  \[\mathrm{PoA}(\mathrm{FPA})\le 2\] 
  (Theorem 3.10).
- For randomized first-price auction rFPA with two bidders per query, 
  \[\mathrm{PoA}(\mathrm{rFPA})\le 1.8\] 
  for a suitable parameter choice (Theorem 4.3).
- For quasi-proportional (power) first-price allocation, 
  \[\mathrm{PoA}\le 2\] 
  as the power parameter \(\alpha\to\infty\) (Theorem 5.1).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for stronger learning-style utility guarantees (e.g., no-regret vs richer benchmark classes like Lipschitz bidding functions, or stochastic/i.i.d. values) that imply strictly better worst-case approximation to optimal liquid welfare in sequential budgeted first-price auctions than guarantees based on best fixed shading multipliers.

This citing paper does not study sequential no-regret learning or benchmarked regret guarantees. Instead it analyzes equilibrium (pure Nash) efficiency in an auto-bidding model with ROS and budget constraints, measuring liquid-welfare PoA against optimal (possibly randomized) allocations. Nevertheless, it is directly relevant in two ways:
- It provides tight lower/upper bounds showing that deterministic FPA can have \(\mathrm{PoA}=n\) when compared to the optimal randomized allocation, due to an \(n\)-factor gap between optimal fractional and integral liquid welfare (Theorem 3.1). This informs what any learning guarantee could hope to imply if the benchmark is fractional Opt.
- It shows that randomization in the mechanism (rFPA, quasi-proportional FPA) can restore constant PoA (\(\le 1.8\) for two bidders; \(\le 2\) for any \(n\) in quasi-proportional limit), suggesting a different route to improved liquid welfare than strengthening bidders’ learning benchmarks.

However, it does not answer whether no-regret against richer bidding-function classes (e.g., Lipschitz \(f\)) or stochastic-value assumptions improve the liquid-welfare guarantees in the sequential-learning model of Fikioris–Tardos; it operates in a different behavioral model (equilibrium of a one-shot multi-query game) and with value-maximizing (ROS) agents rather than quasilinear utility maximizers with \(-\infty\) budget violations.

sources.title: A field guide for pacing budget and ROS constraints
sources.theorem_or_result:
In a stochastic i.i.d. repeated-auction model with a single value-maximizing bidder facing both a hard budget constraint and a return-on-spend (ROS) constraint, the paper analyzes the decentralized “min pacing” bid rule
- bid each round: \(b_t=\min\{\tfrac{1+\lambda_t}{\lambda_t}v_t,\tfrac{1}{\mu_t}v_t,B_t\}\)
with multiplicative-weights updates \(\lambda_{t+1}=\lambda_t\exp(-\alpha(v_tx_t(b_t)-p_t(b_t)))\), \(\mu_{t+1}=\mu_t\exp(-\eta(\rho-p_t(b_t)))\).
Under single-crossing/Lipschitz/strong-monotonicity assumptions on expected constraint gradients, it proves regret \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OPT}-\mathrm{Alg}]=O(\sqrt{T})\) (Theorem 1) and ex-post ROS violation \(\sum_{t\le T}(p_t(b_t)-v_tx_t(b_t))\le O(\sqrt{T}\log T)\) plus budget depletion within \(O(\sqrt{T})\) rounds of the horizon (Theorem 2). It also shows any sequentially-decoupled architecture can incur \(\Omega(T)\) ROS violation or \(\Omega(T)\) regret (Proposition 3.3).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks whether stronger learning-style guarantees (e.g., no-regret to richer benchmarks like Lipschitz bidding functions, or stochastic i.i.d. values) can yield strictly better worst-case liquid-welfare approximations in sequential budgeted first-price auctions than guarantees based on best fixed shading multipliers.

This paper does not analyze liquid welfare or multi-bidder sequential first-price auctions. Instead it studies a single bidder’s online optimization (value maximization) with budget and ROS constraints, in an i.i.d. stochastic environment, and proves \(O(\sqrt{T})\) regret/constraint-violation guarantees for a minimally-coupled pacing algorithm. The relevance is indirect: it provides learning guarantees in the stochastic model and discusses (via related work [LPSZ23]) liquid-welfare efficiency when many such autobidders interact, but it does not itself establish improved liquid-welfare price-of-anarchy bounds under richer benchmark classes (e.g., Lipschitz bidding functions) for the sequential budgeted first-price auction setting of the open problem.

sources.title: Robust Temporal Guarantees in Budgeted Sequential Auctions
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper studies sequential first-price (and some results for second-price) auctions with hard budgets where bidders’ objective is to maximize the total number of wins (all items equally valuable). It proposes the Primal Budget Pacing Algorithm with update 
\[b_i^{(t+1)}=b_i^{(t)}+\eta(\rho_i-p_i^{(t)})\]
where bidder i’s total budget is \(\rho_i T\) (budgets normalized so \(\sum_i \rho_i=1\)). Main guarantees include:
- (Robustness vs adversarial opponents) If a learner with budget \(\rho_L T\) uses the algorithm with \(\eta=1/\sqrt{T}\) and initial bid \(b^{(1)}=\rho_L\), then any (possibly colluding) budget-feasible opponent with budget \(\rho_O T\) can win at most \(\frac{\rho_O}{\rho_L+\rho_O}T+3\sqrt{T}\) rounds (Theorem 3.1), hence the learner wins at least \(\frac{\rho_L}{\rho_L+\rho_O}T-3\sqrt{T}\).
- (Temporal/interval guarantees in self-play) When all agents use the algorithm in first-price auctions, after a burn-in of \(O\big(\frac{1}{\rho_{\min}\eta}\log\frac{1}{\eta}\big)\) rounds, each agent i wins approximately its budget share in every interval of length \(\tau=O(\sqrt{T})\): at least \(\rho_i\tau - O\big(\frac{1}{\rho_{\min}}\big)\) wins (Theorem 4.1). For equal budgets, play becomes round-robin after \(O\big(\frac{n^2}{\eta}\log\frac{1}{\eta}\big)\) rounds (Theorem 5.1).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for stronger learning-style guarantees (e.g., regret vs richer benchmark classes like Lipschitz bidding functions, or stochastic-value models) that imply improved worst-case approximation to optimal liquid welfare \(\mathrm{LW}^*\) in sequential budgeted first-price auctions, beyond guarantees vs a fixed shading multiplier.

This paper does not analyze liquid welfare, utilities \(V_i-P_i\), or regret/competitive ratio against benchmark bidding functions. Instead it changes the objective to “maximize number of wins” (equivalently, all per-round values are identical/constant) and proves robust proportional-win guarantees and strong temporal discrepancy bounds for a specific primal pacing dynamic.

Relevance: the algorithm and analysis provide a new type of robust, adversarial guarantee under budgets (a safety-level proportional share) and a primal-update technique that could be a tool toward welfare guarantees in special cases (e.g., homogeneous values where liquid welfare aligns with number of wins). However, it does not address the stated benchmark-regret-to-liquid-welfare question for general heterogeneous values \(v_{it}\in[0,1]\), nor does it improve known liquid-welfare approximation factors in that general model.

sources.title: Tight Inapproximability for Welfare-Maximizing Autobidding Equilibria
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves tight NP-hardness of improving upon the known liquid-welfare/welfare price-of-anarchy threshold in autobidding settings: for any constant \(\epsilon>0\), it is NP-hard to compute a (second-price) autobidding equilibrium whose welfare is within a factor \(2-\epsilon\) of the welfare-optimal equilibrium (Theorem 1.1 / 3.14). It also shows NP-hardness for relaxed outcomes generated by learning dynamics: for any \(\epsilon>0\), it is NP-hard to compute a “responsive learning sequence” whose average welfare approximates the welfare-optimal one within \(\frac{2e}{2e-1}-\epsilon\) (Theorem 1.8 / 4.9).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether stronger learning-style guarantees (e.g., no-regret vs richer benchmark classes like Lipschitz bidding functions, or stochastic values) can yield strictly better worst-case approximation \(\mathrm{LW}\ge \mathrm{LW}^*/\alpha\) in sequential budgeted first-price auctions than guarantees obtained from comparison to a fixed shading multiplier. This paper does not address sequential first-price auctions with hard budgets, nor does it analyze regret to Lipschitz bidding-function benchmarks. Instead it studies (parallel) second-price autobidding with RoS constraints (often with budgets lifted so liquid welfare equals welfare) and proves computational inapproximability of finding equilibria or learning-generated sequences that beat the baseline PoA-type constants (notably the factor 2 barrier). Thus it provides negative evidence in a related but different model: even if better-than-2 outcomes exist, selecting/computing them can be intractable; and for certain relaxed learning notions, achieving approximation better than \(\approx 2e/(2e-1)\) is NP-hard. This informs the open problem by suggesting barriers to improving worst-case welfare guarantees via stronger behavioral assumptions, though it does not resolve the specific sequential-FPA/Lipschitz-benchmark question.
